meta.title: The RPwD Act 2016
meta.subtitle: Easy Read Guide
meta.audience: A complete reference for persons with intellectual and learning disabilities.
meta.source_note: Section titles verified against the official RPwD Act 2016 (Ministry of Law and Justice).

sections.title: Section 1 — Short title and commencement
sections.plain_text: This section gives the Act its name and tells when it started. The Act began on 19 April 2017 and applies to all of India.
sections.use_when: (For reference. This section is the Act's name plate.)

sections.title: Section 2 — Definitions
sections.plain_text: The Act lists 21 disabilities. If you have one of them, you are covered. If your disability is 40% or more, you are called a person with benchmark disability — you get extra rights.
sections.use_when: You are not sure if the Act covers you, or whether you have a benchmark disability. Look at the list of 21 disabilities and the 40% rule.

sections.title: Section 3 — Equality and non-discrimination
sections.plain_text: You have the right to be treated equally. No one can treat you badly because you have a disability.
sections.use_when: Someone refused you a service, a job, or a place because of your disability. Cite Section 3 in your complaint.

sections.title: Section 4 — Women and children with disabilities
sections.plain_text: Women and children with disabilities have the same rights as everyone else. The government must protect them from extra harm.
sections.use_when: A girl or woman is denied school, work, or safety because of her disability. Or a child is being kept out of school.

sections.title: Section 5 — Community life
sections.plain_text: You have the right to live in your own home and your own community. No one can force you into an institution against your will.
sections.use_when: Someone is forcing a person with a disability to live in a hostel or institution they do not want to live in.

sections.title: Section 6 — Protection from cruelty and inhuman treatment
sections.official_title: Protection from cruelty and inhuman treatment
sections.plain_text: No one can hurt you or treat you in a cruel way. The government must keep you safe.
sections.use_when: A person with a disability is being treated cruelly at home, in school, in a hospital, or in care.

sections.title: Section 7 — Protection from abuse, violence and exploitation
sections.official_title: Protection from abuse, violence and exploitation
sections.plain_text: No one can hit you, hurt you, or use you for their gain. You can tell the police. They must help. You can also ask a magistrate for protection.
sections.use_when: Someone is hurting, abusing, or exploiting a person with a disability.

sections.title: Section 8 — Protection and safety
sections.plain_text: The government must keep persons with disabilities safe in disasters, fires, floods, armed conflict, and other emergencies.
sections.use_when: A relief camp, shelter, or evacuation plan does not include or is not safe for persons with disabilities.

sections.title: Section 9 — Home and family
sections.plain_text: Persons with disabilities have the right to a home and a family. No child can be taken from a parent only because the parent has a disability.
sections.use_when: A child welfare worker tries to take your child away because of your disability.

sections.title: Section 10 — Reproductive rights
sections.plain_text: Persons with disabilities have the right to decide about marriage and children. No one can force you to have an operation that stops you from having children.
sections.use_when: A doctor, family member, or institution wants to sterilise a person with a disability without their full free consent.

sections.title: Section 11 — Accessibility in voting
sections.plain_text: You have the right to vote in private. Polling stations must have ramps, easy ballots, and help if you need it.
sections.use_when: A polling station has no ramp, no Braille ballot, or no help for voters with disabilities.

sections.title: Section 12 — Access to justice
sections.plain_text: You have the right to go to court. Courts must let you take part. They must give you a sign language interpreter or a support person if you need one.
sections.use_when: A court, police station, or tribunal does not let you take part because of your disability.

sections.title: Section 13 — Legal capacity
sections.plain_text: You can make your own decisions. You can own things. You can run a bank account. No one can take that away because of your disability.
sections.use_when: Someone says you cannot sign a document, open a bank account, or own property because of your disability.

sections.title: Section 14 — Provision for guardianship
sections.plain_text: Guardianship can only be a last step. It must be limited and end when you can decide for yourself again. You always have the right to support to make your own choices.
sections.use_when: Someone wants to put a person with a disability under full guardianship without trying support first.

sections.title: Section 15 — Designation of authorities to support
sections.plain_text: The government must name offices that help persons with disabilities make their own decisions.
sections.use_when: (For reference. This section sets up the support system for decision-making.)

sections.title: Section 16 — Duty of educational institutions
sections.plain_text: All schools must welcome children with disabilities. Schools must give the help each child needs to learn.
sections.use_when: A school refused to take a child because of disability, or refuses to give support.

sections.title: Section 17 — Specific measures to promote and facilitate inclusive education
sections.official_title: Specific measures to promote and facilitate inclusive education
sections.plain_text: The government must train teachers, make books in accessible formats, and make schools easy to enter and use.
sections.use_when: A school does not have ramps, accessible books, sign language support, or trained teachers.

sections.title: Section 18 — Adult education
sections.plain_text: The government must run adult education programmes that include adults with disabilities.
sections.use_when: An adult literacy or skills programme has no place for persons with disabilities.

sections.title: Section 19 — Vocational training and self-employment
sections.plain_text: The government must run job training programmes and help persons with disabilities start their own work.
sections.use_when: A skill training programme refused you, or did not have an accessible course.

sections.title: Section 20 — Non-discrimination in employment
sections.plain_text: A government job cannot say no to you because you have a disability. You must be treated fairly at work.
sections.use_when: You did not get a job, a promotion, or a fair review because of your disability.

sections.title: Section 21 — Equal opportunity policy
sections.plain_text: Every workplace with 20 or more workers must write down how they will treat workers with disabilities fairly. They must show this paper if you ask.
sections.use_when: You want to know your workplace's rules for disability support. Ask to see the equal opportunity policy.

sections.title: Section 22 — Maintenance of records
sections.plain_text: Every workplace must keep records of how many workers with disabilities they have, and what support each one needs.
sections.use_when: You ask your workplace for these records and they refuse, or they say no records are kept.

sections.title: Section 23 — Appointment of Grievance Redressal Officer
sections.plain_text: Every government workplace must name an officer to handle complaints from workers with disabilities.
sections.use_when: Your workplace has no Grievance Redressal Officer, or the officer is not doing the job.

sections.title: Section 24 — Social security
sections.plain_text: The government must help you with food, clothes, a home, and other support if you need it to live.
sections.use_when: You need a disability pension, ration card, or housing scheme but were turned away.

sections.title: Section 25 — Healthcare
sections.plain_text: You should get free healthcare close to where you live. Healthcare must work for people with disabilities too.
sections.use_when: A government hospital or clinic refused you, charged you, or had no accessible service.

sections.title: Section 26 — Insurance schemes
sections.plain_text: The government must run insurance schemes for persons with disabilities.
sections.use_when: An insurance company refused you because of your disability, or you cannot find any disability insurance.

sections.title: Section 27 — Rehabilitation
sections.plain_text: The government must run rehabilitation programmes covering health, mind, social skills, and work.
sections.use_when: A government rehab centre is not available, is too far, or refused to take you.

sections.title: Section 28 — Research and development
sections.plain_text: The government must support research on disability, including new tools and treatments.
sections.use_when: (For reference. This section funds research on assistive technology and disability services.)

sections.title: Section 29 — Culture and recreation
sections.plain_text: Persons with disabilities have the right to take part in art, music, films, libraries, and leisure.
sections.use_when: A museum, cinema, library, or cultural event refused you or had no accessible entry.

sections.title: Section 30 — Sporting activities
sections.plain_text: The government must support sports for persons with disabilities.
sections.use_when: A sports facility, training programme, or competition refused to include persons with disabilities.

sections.title: Section 31 — Free education for children with benchmark disabilities
sections.official_title: Free education for children with benchmark disabilities
sections.plain_text: Children with benchmark disabilities aged 6 to 18 have the right to free education in any government or government-aided school.
sections.use_when: A government school asked a child with benchmark disability to pay fees, or refused to admit them.

sections.title: Section 32 — Reservation in higher educational institutions
sections.plain_text: Government colleges and universities must keep 5 seats out of every 100 for students with benchmark disabilities.
sections.use_when: A college denied you a reserved seat or did not have a 5% disability quota in admissions.

sections.title: Section 33 — Identification of posts for reservation
sections.plain_text: The government must list out which jobs are open to persons with benchmark disabilities under the reservation.
sections.use_when: (For reference. This section is the basis for the 4% reservation in Section 34.)

sections.title: Section 34 — Reservation
sections.plain_text: The government must keep 4 jobs out of every 100 for persons with benchmark disabilities. The 4% is split across categories of disability.
sections.use_when: A government office did not follow the 4% job quota, or did not let you apply under the disability category.

sections.title: Section 35 — Incentives to employers in private sector
sections.official_title: Incentives to employers in private sector
sections.plain_text: The government can give private companies incentives like grants and tax breaks if they hire workers with disabilities.
sections.use_when: (For reference. This section encourages private sector hiring of persons with disabilities.)

sections.title: Section 36 — Special employment exchange
sections.plain_text: The government must set up special job centres that help persons with disabilities find work.
sections.use_when: You cannot find a special employment exchange in your district, or the one you found is not helping you.

sections.title: Section 37 — Special schemes and development programmes
sections.plain_text: The government must run schemes that help persons with benchmark disabilities live, study, and work.
sections.use_when: (For reference. This section is the basis for many disability welfare schemes.)

sections.title: Section 38 — Special provisions for persons with disabilities with high support
sections.official_title: Special provisions for persons with disabilities with high support
sections.plain_text: Persons with disabilities who need a lot of support get extra help. They have the right to ask for support, and the help must be reviewed often.
sections.use_when: A person needs daily personal support but is not getting it from any government scheme.

sections.title: Section 39 — Awareness campaigns
sections.plain_text: The government must run programmes to teach people about disability rights and accessibility.
sections.use_when: (For reference. This section requires public awareness of the Act.)

sections.title: Section 40 — Accessibility
sections.plain_text: Buildings, places, transport, and information must be made easy to use for everyone, including persons with disabilities.
sections.use_when: A public building has no ramp, no lift, no accessible toilet, or no clear signs.

sections.title: Section 41 — Access to transport
sections.plain_text: Buses, trains, stations, and airports must have ramps, accessible signs, and trained staff to help.
sections.use_when: A bus, train, or station did not let you board, had no ramp or lift, or had no accessible toilet.

sections.title: Section 42 — Access to information and communication technology
sections.official_title: Access to information and communication technology
sections.plain_text: Websites, apps, TV, and phones must work for people with disabilities. Books, forms, and notices must come in accessible formats too.
sections.use_when: A government website, app, TV news, or printed form is not accessible to you.

sections.title: Section 43 — Consumer goods
sections.plain_text: Things sold in shops — like phones, TVs, and household goods — should be made so that persons with disabilities can use them.
sections.use_when: A product you bought is not usable because of your disability and the maker did not provide an accessible version.

sections.title: Section 44 — Mandatory observance of accessibility norms
sections.plain_text: No new building, transport, or service can get a permit unless it follows the accessibility rules.
sections.use_when: A new building or service has been approved but does not meet the accessibility standards.

sections.title: Section 45 — Time limit for making existing infrastructure and premises accessible and action for that purpose
sections.official_title: Time limit for making existing infrastructure and premises accessible and action for that purpose
sections.plain_text: Old buildings and services must be made accessible within five years. This is the law, not a request.
sections.use_when: A public building or service is still inaccessible long after the law required it to be fixed.

sections.title: Section 46 — Time limit for accessibility by service providers
sections.official_title: Time limit for accessibility by service providers
sections.plain_text: Service providers — like banks, hospitals, and shops — must make their services accessible within a fixed time set by the government.
sections.use_when: A service provider has not made its service accessible within the time the government set.

sections.title: Section 47 — Human resource development
sections.plain_text: The government must train enough teachers, doctors, and other staff to support persons with disabilities.
sections.use_when: There are no trained special educators, sign language interpreters, or rehab workers in your area.

sections.title: Section 48 — Social audit
sections.plain_text: The government must check, at least every five years, if the schemes for persons with disabilities are working — and persons with disabilities themselves should be part of that check.
sections.use_when: A scheme is not being audited, or persons with disabilities were not part of the audit.

sections.title: Section 49 — Competent authority
sections.plain_text: The government must name the office that handles registration of organisations working with persons with disabilities.
sections.use_when: (For reference. This sets up the registration system for disability organisations.)

sections.title: Section 50 — Registration
sections.plain_text: Any institution that wants to work with persons with disabilities must be registered. Without registration, they cannot legally provide services.
sections.use_when: An institution is offering disability services without being registered.

sections.title: Section 51 — Application and grant of certificate of registration
sections.official_title: Application and grant of certificate of registration
sections.plain_text: An institution must apply for a registration certificate. The competent authority gives the certificate after checking that the rules are met.
sections.use_when: Your application for a registration certificate is taking too long, or was unfairly refused.

sections.title: Section 52 — Revocation of registration
sections.plain_text: If a registered institution breaks the rules or harms persons with disabilities, its registration can be cancelled.
sections.use_when: A registered institution is treating persons with disabilities badly. You can ask the authority to cancel its registration.

sections.title: Section 53 — Appeal
sections.plain_text: If your registration is refused or cancelled, you have the right to appeal to a higher authority.
sections.use_when: Your institution's registration was refused or cancelled and you want to challenge that decision.

sections.title: Section 54 — Act not to apply to institutions established or maintained by Central or State Government
sections.official_title: Act not to apply to institutions established or maintained by Central or State Government
sections.plain_text: Government-run institutions for persons with disabilities do not need to register under this chapter — different rules apply to them.
sections.use_when: (For reference. Government institutions are exempt from this registration requirement.)

sections.title: Section 55 — Assistance to registered institutions
sections.plain_text: The government can give grants and other help to registered organisations that serve persons with disabilities.
sections.use_when: (For reference. This section is the basis for funding disability organisations.)

sections.title: Section 56 — Guidelines for assessment of specified disabilities
sections.official_title: Guidelines for assessment of specified disabilities
sections.plain_text: The government must publish clear guidelines on how to assess each of the 21 specified disabilities.
sections.use_when: (For reference. The assessment guidelines are issued by the central government.)

sections.title: Section 57 — Designation of certifying authorities
sections.plain_text: The government must name the doctors and offices that can issue a disability certificate.
sections.use_when: (For reference. This sets up the disability certification system.)

sections.title: Section 58 — Procedure for certification
sections.plain_text: There must be a clear, fair process to get a disability certificate. The certificate is valid all over India.
sections.use_when: A doctor or office is making the certificate process hard, slow, or unclear.

sections.title: Section 59 — Appeal against a decision of certifying authority
sections.official_title: Appeal against a decision of certifying authority
sections.plain_text: If you are refused a disability certificate or get the wrong one, you can appeal.
sections.use_when: Your application for a disability certificate was refused, or the percentage was wrong.

sections.title: Section 60 — Constitution of Central Advisory Board on Disability
sections.official_title: Constitution of Central Advisory Board on Disability
sections.plain_text: The central government must set up a Central Advisory Board on Disability to advise on disability matters.
sections.use_when: (For reference. This sets up the national disability advisory body.)

sections.title: Section 61 — Terms and conditions of service of members
sections.official_title: Terms and conditions of service of members
sections.plain_text: The rules say how Central Advisory Board members are paid and how long they serve.
sections.use_when: (For reference. Administrative rule for Central Advisory Board members.)

sections.title: Section 62 — Disqualifications
sections.plain_text: Some people cannot be members of the Central Advisory Board — for example, people convicted of serious crimes.
sections.use_when: (For reference. Lists who cannot be a Central Advisory Board member.)

sections.title: Section 63 — Vacation of seats by Members
sections.plain_text: If a Central Advisory Board member misses too many meetings or breaks the rules, they lose their seat.
sections.use_when: (For reference. Administrative rule for Central Advisory Board.)

sections.title: Section 64 — Meetings of the Central Advisory Board on disability
sections.official_title: Meetings of the Central Advisory Board on disability
sections.plain_text: The Central Advisory Board must meet at least once every six months.
sections.use_when: (For reference. The Central Advisory Board has not met in over six months.)

sections.title: Section 65 — Functions of Central Advisory Board on disability
sections.official_title: Functions of Central Advisory Board on disability
sections.plain_text: The Central Advisory Board advises the central government on disability rules, schemes, and reports.
sections.use_when: (For reference. Lists what the Central Advisory Board does.)

sections.title: Section 66 — State Advisory Board on disability
sections.plain_text: Every state must set up its own State Advisory Board on Disability.
sections.use_when: Your state does not have a State Advisory Board on Disability.

sections.title: Section 67 — Terms and conditions of service of Members
sections.official_title: Terms and conditions of service of Members
sections.plain_text: Rules for State Advisory Board members — pay, term, and conditions.
sections.use_when: (For reference. Administrative rule for State Advisory Board members.)

sections.title: Section 68 — Disqualification
sections.plain_text: Same kind of rules as the Central Board: who cannot be a State Advisory Board member.
sections.use_when: (For reference. Administrative rule for State Advisory Board.)

sections.title: Section 69 — Vacation of seats
sections.plain_text: When a State Advisory Board member loses their seat — missed meetings, broken rules, etc.
sections.use_when: (For reference. Administrative rule for State Advisory Board.)

sections.title: Section 70 — Meetings of State Advisory Board on disability
sections.official_title: Meetings of State Advisory Board on disability
sections.plain_text: The State Advisory Board must meet at least once every six months.
sections.use_when: Your State Advisory Board has not met in over six months.

sections.title: Section 71 — Functions of State Advisory Board on disability
sections.official_title: Functions of State Advisory Board on disability
sections.plain_text: The State Advisory Board advises the state government on disability rules, schemes, and reports.
sections.use_when: (For reference. Lists what the State Advisory Board does.)

sections.title: Section 72 — District-level Committee on disability
sections.plain_text: Every district must have a committee that handles disability matters at the district level.
sections.use_when: Your district has no disability committee, or the committee is not working.

sections.title: Section 73 — Vacancies not to invalidate proceedings
sections.plain_text: Even if some seats on the Boards or committees are empty, their decisions are still valid.
sections.use_when: (For reference. Administrative rule.)

sections.title: Section 74 — Appointment of Chief Commissioner and Commissioners
sections.official_title: Appointment of Chief Commissioner and Commissioners
sections.plain_text: The central government appoints a Chief Commissioner for Persons with Disabilities and two Commissioners under the Chief Commissioner.
sections.use_when: (For reference. This sets up the national disability complaints office.)

sections.title: Section 75 — Functions of Chief Commissioner
sections.plain_text: The Chief Commissioner watches over how the Act is being followed across India and takes action when rights are broken.
sections.use_when: (For reference. The Chief Commissioner is who you escalate to after the State Commissioner.)

sections.title: Section 76 — Action of appropriate authorities on recommendation of Chief Commissioner
sections.official_title: Action of appropriate authorities on recommendation of Chief Commissioner
sections.plain_text: When the Chief Commissioner makes a recommendation, the government office must act on it within a fixed time and report back.
sections.use_when: The Chief Commissioner gave a recommendation and the office did not act on it.

sections.title: Section 77 — Powers of Chief Commissioner
sections.plain_text: The Chief Commissioner can call witnesses, ask for documents, and act like a civil court when looking into a complaint.
sections.use_when: You filed a complaint with the Chief Commissioner and they have not used their powers.

sections.title: Section 78 — Annual and special reports by Chief Commissioner
sections.official_title: Annual and special reports by Chief Commissioner
sections.plain_text: The Chief Commissioner must publish a yearly report. The report goes to Parliament.
sections.use_when: The Chief Commissioner has not published a yearly report.

sections.title: Section 79 — Appointment of State Commissioner in States
sections.official_title: Appointment of State Commissioner in States
sections.plain_text: Every state must appoint a State Commissioner for Persons with Disabilities.
sections.use_when: Your state does not have a State Commissioner for Persons with Disabilities.

sections.title: Section 80 — Functions of State Commissioner
sections.plain_text: The State Commissioner handles complaints in the state and watches over the Act in the state.
sections.use_when: Use the State Commissioner as your first stop for complaints inside your state.

sections.title: Section 81 — Action by appropriate authorities on recommendation of State Commissioner
sections.official_title: Action by appropriate authorities on recommendation of State Commissioner
sections.plain_text: When the State Commissioner makes a recommendation, the office must act on it and report back within a fixed time.
sections.use_when: The State Commissioner gave a recommendation and the office did not act on it.

sections.title: Section 82 — Powers of State Commissioner
sections.plain_text: The State Commissioner can call witnesses, ask for documents, and act like a civil court when looking into a complaint.
sections.use_when: You filed a complaint and the State Commissioner has not used their powers.

sections.title: Section 83 — Annual and special reports by State Commissioner
sections.official_title: Annual and special reports by State Commissioner
sections.plain_text: The State Commissioner must publish a yearly report. The report goes to the state government.
sections.use_when: The State Commissioner in your state has not published a yearly report.

sections.title: Section 84 — Special Court
sections.plain_text: Each state must have a Special Court to handle serious offences against persons with disabilities under this Act.
sections.use_when: A serious case under this Act is not being heard in a Special Court.

sections.title: Section 85 — Special Public Prosecutor
sections.plain_text: Each Special Court must have a Special Public Prosecutor to argue the case for the victim.
sections.use_when: A case is being heard in the Special Court without a Special Public Prosecutor.

sections.title: Section 86 — National Fund for persons with disabilities
sections.official_title: National Fund for persons with disabilities
sections.plain_text: There is a National Fund for Persons with Disabilities. The Fund pays for schemes, support, and grants.
sections.use_when: (For reference. The Fund pays for many disability programmes.)

sections.title: Section 87 — Accounts and audit
sections.plain_text: The National Fund must keep clear accounts. The accounts must be audited every year.
sections.use_when: (For reference. Audit rule for the National Fund.)

sections.title: Section 88 — State Fund for persons with disabilities
sections.official_title: State Fund for persons with disabilities
sections.plain_text: Every state must set up a State Fund for Persons with Disabilities, like the National Fund.
sections.use_when: Your state has no State Fund, or the State Fund is not being used for persons with disabilities.

sections.title: Section 89 — Punishment for contravention of provisions of Act or rules or regulations made thereunder
sections.official_title: Punishment for contravention of provisions of Act or rules or regulations made thereunder
sections.plain_text: If anyone breaks the rules of this Act, they can be fined. The first time: up to Rs 10,000. The next time: Rs 50,000 to Rs 5 lakh.
sections.use_when: Someone or some office is breaking the rules of this Act and you want them to be punished.

sections.title: Section 90 — Offences by companies
sections.plain_text: If a company breaks the rules of this Act, the people running the company can also be punished.
sections.use_when: A company has broken the rules and you want the managers to be held responsible too.

sections.title: Section 91 — Punishment for fraudulently availing any benefit meant for persons with benchmark disabilities
sections.official_title: Punishment for fraudulently availing any benefit meant for persons with benchmark disabilities
sections.plain_text: If a person fakes a disability to get benefits meant for persons with benchmark disabilities, they can be jailed for up to 2 years and fined up to Rs 1 lakh.
sections.use_when: Someone is faking a disability certificate to get a job, seat, or benefit reserved for persons with disabilities.

sections.title: Section 92 — Punishment for offences of atrocities
sections.plain_text: If someone insults you, hurts you, or humiliates you because of your disability, they can go to jail for 6 months to 5 years.
sections.use_when: Someone publicly insults, beats, denies food, or sexually abuses a person with a disability because of their disability.

sections.title: Section 93 — Punishment for failure to furnish information
sections.official_title: Punishment for failure to furnish information
sections.plain_text: If anyone refuses to give information that the Chief Commissioner or State Commissioner asks for, they can be fined.
sections.use_when: An office refused to send information to the Chief Commissioner or State Commissioner.

sections.title: Section 94 — Previous sanction of appropriate Government
sections.plain_text: Some cases under this Act need permission from the government before they can be filed in court.
sections.use_when: (For reference. Procedural rule for filing certain cases.)

sections.title: Section 95 — Alternative punishments
sections.plain_text: The court can give other punishments — like community service or training — instead of fine or jail in some cases.
sections.use_when: (For reference. Court has flexibility on punishment in some cases.)

sections.title: Section 96 — Application of other laws not barred
sections.official_title: Application of other laws not barred
sections.plain_text: This Act does not stop you from using other laws too. You can use this Act and other laws together.
sections.use_when: (For reference. You can use multiple legal protections at once.)

sections.title: Section 97 — Protection of action taken in good faith
sections.official_title: Protection of action taken in good faith
sections.plain_text: Government officers and others cannot be sued for actions they take honestly under this Act.
sections.use_when: (For reference. Protects officers acting in good faith.)

sections.title: Section 98 — Power to remove difficulties
sections.plain_text: If something in this Act is unclear or hard to do, the central government can issue an order to fix it within three years of the Act starting.
sections.use_when: (For reference. Allows the central government to issue clarifications.)

sections.title: Section 99 — Power to amend Schedule
sections.plain_text: The central government can change the list of 21 specified disabilities — add, remove, or change them.
sections.use_when: (For reference. The list of disabilities can be updated by the central government.)

sections.title: Section 100 — Power of Central Government to make rules
sections.official_title: Power of Central Government to make rules
sections.plain_text: The central government can make rules to put this Act into action.
sections.use_when: (For reference. Many parts of the Act work through rules made under Section 100.)

sections.title: Section 101 — Power of State Government to make rules
sections.official_title: Power of State Government to make rules
sections.plain_text: Each state government can make its own rules to put this Act into action in the state.
sections.use_when: (For reference. State governments make their own rules under Section 101.)

sections.title: Section 102 — Repeal and savings
sections.plain_text: This Act replaces the older 1995 Persons with Disabilities Act, but actions already taken under the old Act are still valid.
sections.use_when: (For reference. The 1995 PWD Act is repealed and replaced by this 2016 Act.)
